does: This paper systematically evaluates DExperts, a decoding-time mitigation technique for reducing toxic outputs in large language models without requiring model retraining. The authors establish baseline toxicity measurements on GPT-2 using RealToxicityPrompts, implement the DExperts method which steers generation at inference time using auxiliary expert models, and evaluate its performance across three phases of testing. The results demonstrate that DExperts achieves near-perfect safety rates (100%) on explicit toxicity benchmarks, but reveals brittleness when stress-tested against implicit hate speech using the adversarial ToxiGen dataset, indicating limitations in handling more subtle toxic patterns that standard benchmarks may miss.
connects: This work builds on growing concerns about toxicity in web-trained language models and extends prior research on both toxicity measurement and mitigation strategies. It directly engages with the RealToxicityPrompts benchmark and related toxicity evaluation frameworks that emerged from earlier studies documenting the absorption of harmful patterns in large-scale training data. The paper replicates and stress-tests DExperts, an inference-time method from the literature on decoding-time steering approaches that aim to modify model behavior without expensive retraining. It connects to broader work on safety-utility tradeoffs in LLMs and adds to the methodological literature on adversarial evaluation of safety techniques, particularly through use of the ToxiGen dataset for testing against implicit harms.
enables: This replication study identifies critical gaps in current toxicity mitigation that should guide future research toward more robust safety mechanisms. By demonstrating DExperts' failure mode against implicit hate speech despite success on explicit toxicity, it motivates development of improved decoding-time methods or training-time approaches that can generalize across explicit and implicit harmful content. The systematic evaluation framework establishes a foundation for comparative studies of other mitigation techniques. The findings suggest future work should focus on benchmark design that captures subtle toxic patterns, exploration of hybrid approaches combining inference-time and training-time strategies, and investigation of why current methods exhibit brittleness under adversarial conditions, ultimately advancing the state of practical safety solutions for LLM deployment.
